    Replaces old_string with new_string in the file.
  terminal(command: str, timeout=None, workdir=None) -> dict
    Foreground only (no background/pty). Returns {"output": "...", "exit_code": N}

Limits: 5-minute timeout, 50KB stdout cap, max 50 tool calls per script. terminal() is foreground-only (no background or pty).

Scripts run in the session's working directory with the active venv's python, so project deps (pandas, etc.) and relative paths work like in terminal().

Print your final result to stdout. Use Python stdlib (json, re, math, csv, datetime, collections, etc.) for processing between tool calls.

Also available (no import needed — built into hermes_tools):
  json_parse(text: str) — json.loads with strict=False; use for terminal() output with control chars
  shell_quote(s: str) — shlex.quote(); use when interpolating dynamic strings into shell commands
  retry(fn, max_attempts=3, delay=2) — retry with exponential backoff for transient failures

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts), 'close' (close stdin/send EOF).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: send_message
function.description:
Send a message to a connected messaging platform, or list available targets.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks to send to a specific channel or person (not just a bare platform name), call send_message(action='list') FIRST to see available targets, then send to the correct one.
If the user just says a platform name like 'send to telegram', send directly to the home channel without listing first.

action.description: Action to perform. 'send' (default) sends a message. 'list' returns all available channels/contacts across connected platforms.

target.description: Delivery target. Format: 'platform' (uses home channel), 'platform:#channel-name', 'platform:chat_id', or 'platform:chat_id:thread_id' for Telegram topics and Discord threads. Examples: 'telegram', 'telegram:-1001234567890:17585', 'discord:999888777:555444333', 'discord:#bot-home', 'slack:#engineering', 'signal:+155****4567', 'matrix:!roomid:server.org', 'matrix:@user:server.org'

function.name: session_search
function.description:
Search your long-term memory of past conversations, or browse recent sessions. This is your recall -- every past session is searchable, and this tool summarizes what happened.

TWO MODES:
1. Recent sessions (no query): Call with no arguments to see what was worked on recently. Returns titles, previews, and timestamps. Zero LLM cost, instant. Start here when the user asks what were we working on or what did we do recently.
2. Keyword search (with query): Search for specific topics across all past sessions. Returns LLM-generated summaries of matching sessions.

USE THIS PROACTIVELY when:
- The user says 'we did this before', 'remember when', 'last time', 'as I mentioned'
- The user asks about a topic you worked on before but don't have in current context
- The user references a project, person, or concept that seems familiar but isn't in memory
- You want to check if you've solved a similar problem before
- The user asks 'what did we do about X?' or 'how did we fix Y?'

Don't hesitate to search when it is actually cross-session -- it's fast and cheap. Better to search and confirm than to guess or ask the user to repeat themselves.

Search syntax: keywords joined with OR for broad recall (elevenlabs OR baseten OR funding), phrases for exact match ("docker networking"), boolean (python NOT java), prefix (deploy*). IMPORTANT: Use OR between keywords for best results — FTS5 defaults to AND which misses sessions that only mention some terms. If a broad OR query returns nothing, try individual keyword searches in parallel. Returns summaries of the top matching sessions.

query.description: Search query — keywords, phrases, or boolean expressions to find in past sessions. Omit this parameter entirely to browse recent sessions instead (returns titles, previews, timestamps with no LLM cost).

role_filter.description: Optional: only search messages from specific roles (comma-separated). E.g. 'user,assistant' to skip tool outputs.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180, foreground max: 600). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily. Foreground timeout above 600s is rejected; use background=true for longer commands.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

watch_patterns.description: Strings to watch for in background process output. Fires a notification the first time each pattern matches a line of output. **Use ONLY for mid-process signals** you want to react to before the process exits — errors, readiness markers, intermediate step markers (e.g. ["ERROR", "Traceback", "listening on port"]). Do NOT use for end-of-run markers (summary headers, 'DONE', 'PASS' printed right before exit) — use `notify_on_complete` for that instead. Stacking end-of-run patterns on top of `notify_on_complete` produces duplicate, delayed notifications that arrive after you've already moved on, since delivery is asynchronous and continues after the process exits.

function.name: text_to_speech
function.description: Convert text to speech audio. Returns a MEDIA: path that the platform delivers as a voice message. On Telegram it plays as a voice bubble, on Discord/WhatsApp as an audio attachment. In CLI mode, saves to ~/voice-memos/. Voice and provider are user-configured, not model-selected.

text.description: The text to convert to speech. Keep under 4000 characters.

output_path.description: Optional custom file path to save the audio. Defaults to ~/.hermes/audio_cache/<timestamp>.mp3

function.name: todo
function.description:
Manage your task list for the current session. Use for complex tasks with 3+ steps or when the user provides multiple tasks. Call with no parameters to read the current list.

Writing:
- Provide 'todos' array to create/update items
- merge=false (default): replace the entire list with a fresh plan
- merge=true: update existing items by id, add any new ones

Each item: {id: string, content: string, status: pending|in_progress|completed|cancelled}
List order is priority. Only ONE item in_progress at a time.
Mark items completed immediately when done. If something fails, cancel it and add a revised item.

Always returns the full current list.

todos.description: Task items to write. Omit to read current list.

merge.description: true: update existing items by id, add new ones. false (default): replace the entire list.

function.description: Analyze images using AI vision. Provides a comprehensive description and answers a specific question about the image content.

image_url.description: Image URL (http/https) or local file path to analyze.

question.description: Your specific question or request about the image to resolve. The AI will automatically provide a complete image description AND answer your specific question.

{"content": "     1|# The Database Cortex Abstraction
     2|## Rethinking Continuous Memory for Multi-Agent Systems
     3|
     4|**Date:** 2026-04-17
     5|**Status:** Draft / Ideation
     6|**Context:** agents-systems-memory
     7|
     8|---
     9|
    10|## 1. The Flawed Paradigm: "Markdown is King"
    11|The current industry consensus treats Markdown files (stored in Git repositories, Obsidian vaults, or static folders) as the ultimate "source of truth" for agent memory. 
    12|
    13|While Markdown is the perfect interface for *human* cognition (readable, diffable, portable), it is the wrong abstraction for a parallel, multi-agent system. To an agent, a Markdown file is a dead, opaque block of text. Searching it requires costly LLM summarization or brittle vector embeddings that lose temporal and relational context. 
    14|
    15|If we treat memory as files, we build a static library. Agents must constantly "read the library" to know what is going on, creating noise, hallucination risks, and massive context bloat.
    16|
    17|## 2. The Cortex Paradigm: Postgres + JSONB
    18|To achieve true "edge" and continuity of meaning (as defined in the Theseus Constitution), the foundational abstraction for memory must be a **Relational Database with JSONB fluidity (PostgreSQL)**.
    19|
    20|- **Relational Columns:** Hard ontology (Agent IDs, Session IDs, Timestamps, Entity relationships, Tags, Status).
    21|- **JSONB Columns:** Fluid cognition (Execution traces, raw thoughts, tool outputs, conversational state, dynamic metadata).
    22|
    23|In this paradigm, memory is not a place where agents *write* documents; memory is the continuous stream of structured exhaust that the system *emits*.
    24|
    25|## 3. Continuous Memory Processes (The Biological Analogy)
    26|
    27|By structuring memory in a database, we unlock the ability to deploy independent, specialized agents whose sole purpose is to manage the system's "mind". 
    28|
    29|### A. The Active Layer (The Waking State)
    30|- **Execution Agents** (Zeus, Theseus, Prometheus, OpenClaw): Operate in the real world. They don't write markdown journals. They stream their thoughts, tool calls, errors, and outcomes as JSONB rows into the `mothership` DB in real-time.
    31|- **Result:** High-fidelity, granular, short-term episodic memory.
    32|
    33|### B. The Subconscious Layer (The Dreaming Agents)
    34|Operating continuously in the background (the system's "sleep" or idle cycles), specialized agents read the recent DB rows and perform cognitive consolidation:
    35|- **Consolidation Agents:** Compress verbose execution logs into dense, semantic summaries. They transition data from *Short-Term Memory* to *Long-Term Memory*.
    36|- **Dreaming / Pattern-Matching Agents:** Look across multiple sessions to find correlations. *"Theseus failed 3 times on Traefik configurations this week. Zeus mentioned a similar issue. Let's extract a global rule/skill."*
    37|- **Emotional / Sentiment Agents:** Parse logs to extract implicit states:
    38|  - **Fears/Risks:** "The human mission owner expressed doubt about the scalability of the broker."
    39|  - **Opportunities:** "There is redundant code in the WASM runner that could be abstracted."
    40|
    41|### C. The Injection Layer (The Reflexes)
    42|When a human summons an agent, or the system dispatches a worker, they do not start with a blank slate or a generic `context.md`.
    43|- **Context Injection Agents:** Query the consolidated database to assemble a bespoke, hyper-relevant JSON payload (The State of the World + Relevant Long-Term Memories + Active Risks) and inject it into the prompt. 
    44|
    45|## 4. Conclusion
    46|Shifting from a "Markdown Archive" to a "Database Cortex" allows memory to become active rather than passive. Processes of classification, consolidation, and context injection become independent actors in the system. This is the architectural leap required to stop managing scripts and start nurturing a continuous, thinking system.", "total_lines": 45, "file_size": 3739, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Esboço: Database Cortex (PostgreSQL + JSONB)
     2|
     3|Este é um documento de trabalho para desenhar o esquema relacional da memória dos agentes, focado em duas tabelas principais.
     4|
     5|## 1. O Rio do Tempo (`event_stream`)
     6|Um *append-only log* para todos os estímulos e pensamentos.
     7|
     8|```sql
     9|CREATE TABLE event_stream (
    10|    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    11|    timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    12|    source VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- Ex: 'telegram', 'teams', 'email', 'agent_hermes'
    13|    event_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- Ex: 'message_received', 'tool_call', 'webhook_trigger'
    14|    actor_id VARCHAR(100), -- Quem gerou o evento (ID do utilizador ou do agente)
    15|    payload JSONB NOT NULL -- O conteúdo real do evento (o corpo do email, a mensagem, etc)
    16|);
    17|
    18|-- Índice crucial para ordenar o tempo
    19|CREATE INDEX idx_event_stream_timestamp ON event_stream(timestamp);
    20|-- Índice GIN para pesquisas flexíveis no JSONB
    21|CREATE INDEX idx_event_stream_payload ON event_stream USING GIN (payload);
    22|```
    23|
    24|## 2. A Mesa de Trabalho (`active_dossiers`)
    25|A memória curta, atualizada de forma assíncrona com base no `event_stream`.
    26|
    27|```sql
    28|CREATE TABLE active_dossiers (
    29|    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    30|    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    31|    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    32|    title VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    33|    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open', -- 'open', 'resolved', 'archived'
    34|    context JSONB NOT NULL -- Estado atual da tarefa, sumários, e IDs de eventos relacionados
    35|);
    36|
    37|CREATE INDEX idx_active_dossiers_status ON active_dossiers(status);
    38|```
    39|
    40|## 3. Vias de Arquitetura de Recolha (Ingestão de Dados)
    41|
    42|Discutimos três abordagens principais para alimentar o `event_stream`. A arquitetura ideal deverá ser um híbrido entre a Via 2 e a Via 3.
    43|
    44|### Via 1: Checkouts Manuais (Frágil)
    45|- **Como funciona:** O agente, no final de cada tarefa ou passo, é responsável por fazer um "checkout" e gravar na base de dados.
    46|- **Problema:** Exige disciplina da "mente consciente" do agente. Se o agente falhar, for interrompido ou "morrer", o contexto e a experiência perdem-se irremediavelmente. Não garante um registo temporal contínuo.
    47|
    48|### Via 2: Sincronização Assíncrona de Ficheiros (Processamento Noturno)
    49|- **Como funciona:** Ações, logs e outputs são escritos naturalmente no sistema de ficheiros ou num *buffer*. Um "Agente de Consolidação" (como o processo de sonho) varre periodicamente estes ficheiros, estrutura a informação e injeta-a no PostgreSQL, organizando os dossiês por tempo.
    50|- **Vantagem:** Separa a ação primária da consolidação. Garante resiliência e não bloqueia a execução normal das tarefas.
    51|
    52|### Via 3: O Estado Infraestrutural Absoluto (Gravação do Pod)
    53|- **A Ideia Original (O Snapshot):** Como disseste, a abordagem mais extrema da persistência seria gravar o próprio *Pod* em execução (a memória RAM, o estado do disco no container, os processos ativos). Se o agente é efémero e pode mudar de tecnologia (de Hermes para outro amanhã), a única forma de garantir que *zero* contexto se perde é "congelar" a própria infraestrutura onde ele corre no momento.
    54|- **Como funcionaria:** O sistema Kubernetes (o barco Theseus) ou o orquestrador faria *snapshots* regulares do estado completo do Pod ou do seu disco persistente e metadados, anexando a isso um carimbo de tempo. Se o Pod morrer, pode ser ressuscitado exatamente no mesmo ponto neurológico.
    55|- **Desafio vs Vantagem:** A vantagem é a fidelidade absoluta (o agente nunca percebe que morreu e acordou noutro corpo). O desafio é que este "estado" não é facilmente pesquisável. Não é um log ordenado e estruturado num PostgreSQL; é um bloco binário ou um *dump* de ficheiros. Torna a memória persistente para *sobrevivência*, mas não para *pesquisa, consolidação ou abstração* (como a criação de dossiês).
    56|
    57|### Via 4: O Híbrido Causal (Interceção do Fluxo de Informação)
    58|- **Como funciona:** Em vez de gravar o *Pod* todo (Via 3), focamo-nos na *Comunicação* que entra e sai dele. Capturamos passivamente todo o "event stream" ao nível do sistema — prompts, inputs, chamadas de ferramentas, mensagens do Telegram/Teams — antes de entrarem no agente e depois de saírem, injetando esse texto/JSON bruto no PostgreSQL. 
    59|- **Vantagem:** É o registo inabalável da experiência. Separa a "memória biográfica" do "corpo atual". Mesmo que o Pod mude ou a tecnologia do agente seja reescrita, a história inteira de *inputs* e *outputs* está no PostgreSQL, pronta a ser lida pelo novo agente. É agnóstico da tecnologia e pesquisável.
    60|
    61|---
    62|
    63|## Próximos Passos (A Explorar)
    64|- Como estruturamos o `payload` JSONB para um Email vs Mensagem do Teams?
    65|- O Agente de Memória fará polling do `event_stream` ou usamos *Listen/Notify* do Postgres?

{"content": "     1|# Configuração Via 4 - Traefik HTTP Traffic Mirroring
     2|
     3|Este documento detalha a configuração do Traefik para implementar a arquitetura **Via 4**, que consiste num Reverse Proxy que recebe pedidos HTTP, encaminha-os para o serviço principal (`agent`) e cria uma cópia exata do request (Traffic Mirroring/Shadowing) para um serviço secundário (`ingestion`).
     4|
     5|O Traefik suporta *Mirroring* nativamente através da definição de serviços do tipo `mirroring`. A percentagem de pedidos espelhados pode ser controlada (neste caso, 100%).
     6|
     7|Abaixo são apresentadas duas abordagens: uma baseada totalmente em **Docker Labels** (recomendada em ambientes de contentores) e outra baseada em **Ficheiros de Configuração** (`traefik.yml` e `dynamic.yml`).
     8|
     9|---
    10|
    11|## Opção 1: Configuração via Docker Compose (Labels)
    12|
    13|A forma mais simples e dinâmica de configurar o Traefik num ambiente Docker é através de *Labels* diretamente no `docker-compose.yml`.
    14|
    15|```yaml
    16|version: '3.8'
    17|
    18|services:
    19|  traefik:
    20|    image: traefik:v3.0 # Pode ser usada a v2.x também
    21|    command:
    22|      - "--api.insecure=true" # Dashboard disponível no porto 8080 (apenas para dev)
    23|      - "--providers.docker=true"
    24|      - "--providers.docker.exposedbydefault=false"
    25|      - "--entrypoints.web.address=:80" # Recebe chamadas HTTP em texto limpo
    26|    ports:
    27|      - "80:80"     # Porta para os pedidos HTTP
    28|      - "8080:8080" # Porta para o Dashboard do Traefik
    29|    volumes:
    30|      - "/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro"
    31|
    32|  agent:
    33|    image: containous/whoami # Substituir pela imagem real do serviço principal
    34|    labels:
    35|      - "traefik.enable=true"
    36|      
    37|      # 1. Roteamento: Todos os pedidos entram por aqui
    38|      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.rule=PathPrefix(`/`)"
    39|      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.entrypoints=web"
    40|      
    41|      # 2. Ligar o router ao serviço de mirroring
    42|      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.service=via4-mirror-service"
    43|      
    44|      # 3. Definição do serviço de Mirroring
    45|      # O serviço principal que vai responder ao pedido
    46|      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.service=agent-service"
    47|      # O serviço secundário que vai receber a cópia (mirror)
    48|      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.mirrors[0].name=ingestion-service"
    49|      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.mirrors[0].percent=100" # Copiar 100% do tráfego
    50|      
    51|      # 4. Definição do serviço principal (agent)
    52|      - "traefik.http.services.agent-service.loadbalancer.server.port=80" # Porta interna do agent
    53|
    54|  ingestion:
    55|    image: containous/whoami # Substituir pela imagem real do microserviço secundário
    56|    labels:
    57|      - "traefik.enable=true"
    58|      # Definição do serviço secundário (usado acima na configuração de mirroring)
    59|      - "traefik.http.services.ingestion-service.loadbalancer.server.port=80" # Porta interna do ingestion
    60|```
    61|
    62|---
    63|
    64|## Opção 2: Configuração via Ficheiros (File Provider)
    65|
    66|Se os serviços não estiverem em Docker ou for preferível usar ficheiros, é necessário ter o `traefik.yml` (configuração estática) e um `dynamic.yml` (configuração dinâmica com os routers e services).
    67|
    68|### 1. `docker-compose.yml`
    69|
    70|```yaml
    71|version: '3.8'
    72|
    73|services:
    74|  traefik:
    75|    image: traefik:v3.0
    76|    ports:
    77|      - "80:80"
    78|      - "8080:8080"
    79|    volumes:
    80|      - ./traefik.yml:/etc/traefik/traefik.yml:ro
    81|      - ./dynamic.yml:/etc/traefik/dynamic.yml:ro
    82|
    83|  agent:
    84|    image: containous/whoami
    85|    ports:
    86|      - "8081:80" # Exposto apenas para demonstração do file provider
    87|      
    88|  ingestion:
    89|    image: containous/whoami
    90|    ports:
    91|      - "8082:80"
    92|```
    93|
    94|### 2. `traefik.yml` (Configuração Estática)
    95|
    96|```yaml
    97|entryPoints:
    98|  web:
    99|    address: ":80"
   100|
   101|api:
   102|  insecure: true
   103|
   104|providers:
   105|  file:
   106|    filename: "/etc/traefik/dynamic.yml"
   107|    watch: true
   108|```
   109|
   110|### 3. `dynamic.yml` (Configuração Dinâmica de Mirroring)
   111|
   112|```yaml
   113|http:
   114|  routers:
   115|    via4-router:
   116|      rule: "PathPrefix(`/`)"
   117|      entryPoints:
   118|        - "web"
   119|      service: "via4-mirror-service"
   120|
   121|  services:
   122|    # O serviço de Mirroring
   123|    via4-mirror-service:
   124|      mirroring:
   125|        service: "agent-service" # Serviço que devolve a resposta real ao cliente
   126|        mirrors:
   127|          - name: "ingestion-service" # Serviço que recebe o shadow traffic
   128|            percent: 100
   129|
   130|    # O serviço principal real
   131|    agent-service:
   132|      loadBalancer:
   133|        servers:
   134|          - url: "http://agent:80" # URL ou IP do container agent
   135|
   136|    # O serviço secundário real (Shadowing)
   137|    ingestion-service:
   138|      loadBalancer:
   139|        servers:
   140|          - url: "http://ingestion:80" # URL ou IP do container ingestion
   141|```
   142|
   143|## Notas Adicionais sobre o Mirroring no Traefik:
   144|1. **Respostas Ignoradas:** As respostas do serviço definido como mirror (`ingestion`) são descartadas. Apenas a resposta do serviço principal (`agent`) é devolvida ao cliente original.
   145|2. **Impacto na Performance:** O mirroring do Traefik envia os requests em "fire-and-forget" assíncrono para os mirrors. Se o serviço de `ingestion` falhar ou estiver lento, o cliente original não deverá ser bloqueado nem notará diferenças na latência da resposta do `agent`.
   146|3. **MaxBodySize:** Nas versões mais recentes pode ser necessário configurar a opção `maxBodySize` dentro das definições do espelho se for esperado lidar com corpos de pedidos muito pesados, visto que o proxy tem de colocar o corpo em memória para o duplicar.
   147|", "total_lines": 146, "file_size": 5490, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

The Flawed Paradigm: \\\"Markdown is King\\\"\\nThe current industry consensus treats Markdown files (stored in Git repositories, Obsidian vaults, or static folders) as the ultimate \\\"source of truth\\\" for agent memory. \\n\\nWhile Markdown is the perfect interface for *human* cognition (readable, diffable, portable), it is the wrong abstraction for a parallel, multi-agent system. To an agent, a Markdown file is a dead, opaque block of text. Searching it requires costly LLM summarization or brittle vector embeddings that lose temporal and relational context. \\n\\nIf we treat memory as files, we build a static library. Agents must constantly \\\"read the library\\\" to know what is going on, creating noise, hallucination risks, and massive context bloat.\\n\\n### 2. The Cortex Paradigm: Postgres + JSONB\\nTo achieve true \\\"edge\\\" and continuity of meaning (as defined in the Theseus Constitution), the foundational abstraction for memory must be a **Relational Database with JSONB fluidity (PostgreSQL)**.\\n\\n- **Relational Columns:** Hard ontology (Agent IDs, Session IDs, Timestamps, Entity relationships, Tags, Status).\\n- **JSONB Columns:** Fluid cognition (Execution traces, raw thoughts, tool outputs, conversational state, dynamic metadata).\\n\\nIn this paradigm, memory is not a place where agents *write* documents; memory is the continuous stream of structured exhaust that the system *emits*.\\n\\n### 3. Continuous Memory Processes (The Biological Analogy)\\n\\nBy structuring memory in a database, we unlock the ability to deploy independent, specialized agents whose sole purpose is to manage the system's \\\"mind\\\". \\n\\n#### A. The Active Layer (The Waking State)\\n- **Execution Agents** (Zeus, Theseus, Prometheus, OpenClaw): Operate in the real world. They don't write markdown journals. They stream their thoughts, tool calls, errors, and outcomes as JSONB rows into the `mothership` DB in real-time.\\n- **Result:** High-fidelity, granular, short-term episodic memory.\\n\\n#### B. The Subconscious Layer (The Dreaming Agents)\\nOperating continuously in the background (the system's \\\"sleep\\\" or idle cycles), specialized agents read the recent DB rows and perform cognitive consolidation:\\n- **Consolidation Agents:** Compress verbose execution logs into dense, semantic summaries. They transition data from *Short-Term Memory* to *Long-Term Memory*.\\n- **Dreaming / Pattern-Matching Agents:** Look across multiple sessions to find correlations. *\\\"Theseus failed 3 times on Traefik configurations this week. Zeus mentioned a similar issue. Let's extract a global rule/skill.\\\"*\\n- **Emotional / Sentiment Agents:** Parse logs to extract implicit states:\\n  - **Fears/Risks:** \\\"The human mission owner expressed doubt about the scalability of the broker.\\\"\\n  - **Opportunities:** \\\"There is redundant code in the WASM runner that could be abstracted.\\\"\\n\\n#### C. Conclusion\\nShifting from a \\\"Markdown Archive\\\" to a \\\"Database Cortex\\\" allows memory to become active rather than passive. Processes of classification, consolidation, and context injection become independent actors in the system. This is the architectural leap required to stop managing scripts and start nurturing a continuous, thinking system.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 02 - Esboço: Database Cortex (PostgreSQL + JSONB)\\n\\nEste é um documento de trabalho para desenhar o esquema relacional da memória dos agentes, focado em duas tabelas principais.\\n\\n### 1. title: The Database Cortex Abstraction

# The Database Cortex Abstraction
## Rethinking Continuous Memory for Multi-Agent Systems

**Date:** 2026-04-17
**Status:** Draft / Ideation
**Context:** agents-systems-memory

---

## 1. The Flawed Paradigm: "Markdown is King"
The current industry consensus treats Markdown files (stored in Git repositories, Obsidian vaults, or static folders) as the ultimate "source of truth" for agent memory. 

While Markdown is the perfect interface for *human* cognition (readable, diffable, portable), it is the wrong abstraction for a parallel, multi-agent system. To an agent, a Markdown file is a dead, opaque block of text. Searching it requires costly LLM summarization or brittle vector embeddings that lose temporal and relational context. 

If we treat memory as files, we build a static library. Agents must constantly "read the library" to know what is going on, creating noise, hallucination risks, and massive context bloat.

## 2. The Cortex Paradigm: Postgres + JSONB
To achieve true "edge" and continuity of meaning (as defined in the Theseus Constitution), the foundational abstraction for memory must be a **Relational Database with JSONB fluidity (PostgreSQL)**.

- **Relational Columns:** Hard ontology (Agent IDs, Session IDs, Timestamps, Entity relationships, Tags, Status).
- **JSONB Columns:** Fluid cognition (Execution traces, raw thoughts, tool outputs, conversational state, dynamic metadata).

In this paradigm, memory is not a place where agents *write* documents; memory is the continuous stream of structured exhaust that the system *emits*.

## 3. Continuous Memory Processes (The Biological Analogy)

By structuring memory in a database, we unlock the ability to deploy independent, specialized agents whose sole purpose is to manage the system's "mind". 

### A. The Active Layer (The Waking State)
- **Execution Agents** (Zeus, Theseus, Prometheus, OpenClaw): Operate in the real world. They don't write markdown journals. They stream their thoughts, tool calls, errors, and outcomes as JSONB rows into the `mothership` DB in real-time.
- **Result:** High-fidelity, granular, short-term episodic memory.

### B. The Subconscious Layer (The Dreaming Agents)
Operating continuously in the background (the system's "sleep" or idle cycles), specialized agents read the recent DB rows and perform cognitive consolidation:
- **Consolidation Agents:** Compress verbose execution logs into dense, semantic summaries. They transition data from *Short-Term Memory* to *Long-Term Memory*.
- **Dreaming / Pattern-Matching Agents:** Look across multiple sessions to find correlations. *"Theseus failed 3 times on Traefik configurations this week. Zeus mentioned a similar issue. Let's extract a global rule/skill."*
- **Emotional / Sentiment Agents:** Parse logs to extract implicit states:
  - **Fears/Risks:** "The human mission owner expressed doubt about the scalability of the broker."
  - **Opportunities:** "There is redundant code in the WASM runner that could be abstracted."

### C. The Injection Layer (The Reflexes)
When a human summons an agent, or the system dispatches a worker, they do not start with a blank slate or a generic `context.md`.
- **Context Injection Agents:** Query the consolidated database to assemble a bespoke, hyper-relevant JSON payload (The State of the World + Relevant Long-Term Memories + Active Risks) and inject it into the prompt. 

## 4. Conclusion
Shifting from a "Markdown Archive" to a "Database Cortex" allows memory to become active rather than passive. Processes of classification, consolidation, and context injection become independent actors in the system. This is the architectural leap required to stop managing scripts and start nurturing a continuous, thinking system.","path":"/home/hugo/office-personal/archive/agents-systems-memory/01-db-cortex-abstraction.md"}

# Esboço: Database Cortex (PostgreSQL + JSONB)

Este é um documento de trabalho para desenhar o esquema relacional da memória dos agentes, focado em duas tabelas principais.

## 1. O Rio do Tempo (`event_stream`)
Um *append-only log* para todos os estímulos e pensamentos.

```sql
CREATE TABLE event_stream (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    source VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- Ex: 'telegram', 'teams', 'email', 'agent_hermes'
    event_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- Ex: 'message_received', 'tool_call', 'webhook_trigger'
    actor_id VARCHAR(100), -- Quem gerou o evento (ID do utilizador ou do agente)
    payload JSONB NOT NULL -- O conteúdo real do evento (o corpo do email, a mensagem, etc)
);

-- Índice crucial para ordenar o tempo
CREATE INDEX idx_event_stream_timestamp ON event_stream(timestamp);
-- Índice GIN para pesquisas flexíveis no JSONB
CREATE INDEX idx_event_stream_payload ON event_stream USING GIN (payload);
```

## 2. A Mesa de Trabalho (`active_dossiers`)
A memória curta, atualizada de forma assíncrona com base no `event_stream`.

```sql
CREATE TABLE active_dossiers (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    title VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open', -- 'open', 'resolved', 'archived'
    context JSONB NOT NULL -- Estado atual da tarefa, sumários, e IDs de eventos relacionados
);

CREATE INDEX idx_active_dossiers_status ON active_dossiers(status);
```

## 3. Vias de Arquitetura de Recolha (Ingestão de Dados)

Discutimos três abordagens principais para alimentar o `event_stream`. A arquitetura ideal deverá ser um híbrido entre a Via 2 e a Via 3.

### Via 1: Checkouts Manuais (Frágil)
- **Como funciona:** O agente, no final de cada tarefa ou passo, é responsável por fazer um "checkout" e gravar na base de dados.
- **Problema:** Exige disciplina da "mente consciente" do agente. Se o agente falhar, for interrompido ou "morrer", o contexto e a experiência perdem-se irremediavelmente. Não garante um registo temporal contínuo.

### Via 2: Sincronização Assíncrona de Ficheiros (Processamento Noturno)
- **Como funciona:** Ações, logs e outputs são escritos naturalmente no sistema de ficheiros ou num *buffer*. Um "Agente de Consolidação" (como o processo de sonho) varre periodicamente estes ficheiros, estrutura a informação e injeta-a no PostgreSQL, organizando os dossiês por tempo.
- **Vantagem:** Separa a ação primária da consolidação. Garante resiliência e não bloqueia a execução normal das tarefas.

### Via 3: O Estado Infraestrutural Absoluto (Gravação do Pod)
- **A Ideia Original (O Snapshot):** Como disseste, a abordagem mais extrema da persistência seria gravar o próprio *Pod* em execução (a memória RAM, o estado do disco no container, os processos ativos). Se o agente é efémero e pode mudar de tecnologia (de Hermes para outro amanhã), a única forma de garantir que *zero* contexto se perde é "congelar" a própria infraestrutura onde ele corre no momento.
- **Como funcionaria:** O sistema Kubernetes (o barco Theseus) ou o orquestrador faria *snapshots* regulares do estado completo do Pod ou do seu disco persistente e metadados, anexando a isso um carimbo de tempo. Se o Pod morrer, pode ser ressuscitado exatamente no mesmo ponto neurológico.
- **Desafio vs Vantagem:** A vantagem é a fidelidade absoluta (o agente nunca percebe que morreu e acordou noutro corpo). O desafio é que este "estado" não é facilmente pesquisável. Não é um log ordenado e estruturado num PostgreSQL; é um bloco binário ou um *dump* de ficheiros. Torna a memória persistente para *sobrevivência*, mas não para *pesquisa, consolidação ou abstração* (como a criação de dossiês).

### Via 4: O Híbrido Causal (Interceção do Fluxo de Informação)
- **Como funciona:** Em vez de gravar o *Pod* todo (Via 3), focamo-nos na *Comunicação* que entra e sai dele. Capturamos passivamente todo o "event stream" ao nível do sistema — prompts, inputs, chamadas de ferramentas, mensagens do Telegram/Teams — antes de entrarem no agente e depois de saírem, injetando esse texto/JSON bruto no PostgreSQL. 
- **Vantagem:** É o registo inabalável da experiência. Separa a "memória biográfica" do "corpo atual". Mesmo que o Pod mude ou a tecnologia do agente seja reescrita, a história inteira de *inputs* e *outputs* está no PostgreSQL, pronta a ser lida pelo novo agente. É agnóstico da tecnologia e pesquisável.

---

## Próximos Passos (A Explorar)
- Como estruturamos o `payload` JSONB para um Email vs Mensagem do Teams?
- O Agente de Memória fará polling do `event_stream` ou usamos *Listen/Notify* do Postgres?","path":"/home/hugo/office-personal/archive/agents-systems-memory/02-esboco-db-cortex.md"}

---
# Configuração Via 4 - Traefik HTTP Traffic Mirroring

Este documento detalha a configuração do Traefik para implementar a arquitetura **Via 4**, que consiste num Reverse Proxy que recebe pedidos HTTP, encaminha-os para o serviço principal (`agent`) e cria uma cópia exata do request (Traffic Mirroring/Shadowing) para um serviço secundário (`ingestion`).

O Traefik suporta *Mirroring* nativamente através da definição de serviços do tipo `mirroring`. A percentagem de pedidos espelhados pode ser controlada (neste caso, 100%).

Abaixo são apresentadas duas abordagens: uma baseada totalmente em **Docker Labels** (recomendada em ambientes de contentores) e outra baseada em **Ficheiros de Configuração** (`traefik.yml` e `dynamic.yml`).

---

## Opção 1: Configuração via Docker Compose (Labels)

A forma mais simples e dinâmica de configurar o Traefik num ambiente Docker é através de *Labels* diretamente no `docker-compose.yml`.

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.0 # Pode ser usada a v2.x também
    command:
      - "--api.insecure=true" # Dashboard disponível no porto 8080 (apenas para dev)
      - "--providers.docker=true"
      - "--providers.docker.exposedbydefault=false"
      - "--entrypoints.web.address=:80" # Recebe chamadas HTTP em texto limpo
    ports:
      - "80:80"     # Porta para os pedidos HTTP
      - "8080:8080" # Porta para o Dashboard do Traefik
    volumes:
      - "/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro"

  agent:
    image: containous/whoami # Substituir pela imagem real do serviço principal
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      
      # 1. Roteamento: Todos os pedidos entram por aqui
      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.rule=PathPrefix(`/`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.entrypoints=web"
      
      # 2. Ligar o router ao serviço de mirroring
      - "traefik.http.routers.via4-router.service=via4-mirror-service"
      
      # 3. Definição do serviço de Mirroring
      # O serviço principal que vai responder ao pedido
      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.service=agent-service"
      # O serviço secundário que vai receber a cópia (mirror)
      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.mirrors[0].name=ingestion-service"
      - "traefik.http.services.via4-mirror-service.mirroring.mirrors[0].percent=100" # Copiar 100% do tráfego
      
      # 4. Definição do serviço principal (agent)
      - "traefik.http.services.agent-service.loadbalancer.server.port=80" # Porta interna do agent

  ingestion:
    image: containous/whoami # Substituir pela imagem real do microserviço secundário
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      # Definição do serviço secundário (usado acima na configuração de mirroring)
      - "traefik.http.services.ingestion-service.loadbalancer.server.port=80" # Porta interna do ingestion
```

---

## Opção 2: Configuração via Ficheiros (File Provider)

Se os serviços não estiverem em Docker ou for preferível usar ficheiros, é necessário ter o `traefik.yml` (configuração estática) e um `dynamic.yml` (configuração dinâmica com os routers e services).

### 1. `docker-compose.yml`

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.0
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - ./traefik.yml:/etc/traefik/traefik.yml:ro
      - ./dynamic.yml:/etc/traefik/dynamic.yml:ro

  agent:
    image: containous/whoami
    ports:
      - "8081:80" # Exposto apenas para demonstração do file provider
      
  ingestion:
    image: containous/whoami
    ports:
      - "8082:80"
```

### 2. `traefik.yml` (Configuração Estática)

```yaml
entryPoints:
  web:
    address: ":80"

api:
  insecure: true

providers:
  file:
    filename: "/etc/traefik/dynamic.yml"
    watch: true
```

### 3. `dynamic.yml` (Configuração Dinâmica de Mirroring)

```yaml
http:
  routers:
    via4-router:
      rule: "PathPrefix(`/`)"
      entryPoints:
        - "web"
      service: "via4-mirror-service"

  services:
    # O serviço de Mirroring
    via4-mirror-service:
      mirroring:
        service: "agent-service" # Serviço que devolve a resposta real ao cliente
        mirrors:
          - name: "ingestion-service" # Serviço que recebe o shadow traffic
            percent: 100

    # O serviço principal real
    agent-service:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://agent:80" # URL ou IP do container agent

    # O serviço secundário real (Shadowing)
    ingestion-service:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://ingestion:80" # URL ou IP do container ingestion
```

## Notas Adicionais sobre o Mirroring no Traefik:
1. **Respostas Ignoradas:** As respostas do serviço definido como mirror (`ingestion`) são descartadas. Apenas a resposta do serviço principal (`agent`) é devolvida ao cliente original.
2. **Impacto na Performance:** O mirroring do Traefik envia os requests em "fire-and-forget" assíncrono para os mirrors. Se o serviço de `ingestion` falhar ou estiver lento, o cliente original não deverá ser bloqueado nem notará diferenças na latência da resposta do `agent`.
3. **MaxBodySize:** Nas versões mais recentes pode ser necessário configurar a opção `maxBodySize` dentro das definições do espelho se for esperado lidar com corpos de pedidos muito pesados, visto que o proxy tem de colocar o corpo em memória para o duplicar.","path":"/home/hugo/office-personal/archive/agents-systems-memory/03-via4-proxy-config.md"}

{"content":"# Memória Contínua e Sistemas Multi-Agente: O Paradigma Cortex

Este documento é a síntese do modelo conceptual e arquitetónico para a memória num sistema de agentes paralelos.

## 1. O Paradigma Cortex (PostgreSQL + JSONB)

A visão atual assenta na transição do "Markdown Archive" (arquivos de texto estáticos) para uma "Database Cortex", em que a memória deixa de ser passiva e passa a ser uma base de dados relacional (PostgreSQL) com forte fluidez de dados (JSONB).

**Tabela: O Rio do Tempo (`event_stream`)**
Um registo imutável (append-only) de todo o fluxo causal do sistema.
*   **Colunas Chave:** `id`, `timestamp`, `source`, `event_type`, `actor_id`, `payload` (JSONB).
*   **Índices:** Ordenação rigorosa por `timestamp` e indexação GIN sobre o `payload` JSONB para pesquisas flexíveis no conteúdo abstrato das interações.

**Tabela: A Mesa de Trabalho (`active_dossiers`)**
A memória a curto-prazo, correspondente às tarefas em curso, atualizada assincronamente a partir do Rio do Tempo.
*   **Colunas Chave:** `id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `title`, `status`, `context` (JSONB).

## 2. Processos de Memória Contínua (Analogia Biológica)

Ao tratarmos a memória como um fluxo estruturado na DB, podemos isolar os agentes por funções cognitivas:

*   **A Camada Ativa (Waking State):** Agentes de execução operam e escrevem fluxos de pensamento, ações (tool calls) e erros de volta para a DB (no seu "Rio do Tempo").
*   **A Camada Subconsciente (Dreaming Agents):** Agentes especializados consolidadam assincronamente os dados verbosos num formato conciso, fazem correspondência de padrões ao longo de múltiplas sessões (ex. deduções de "skills" ou deteção de erros recorrentes) e filtram dados sentimentais.
*   **A Camada de Injeção (Reflexes):** Agentes de injeção de contexto montam uma payload de estado atual (do dossier) e injetam-na na prompt quando um agente é invocado.

## 3. Arquitetura de Ingestão de Dados (A Via 4)

A arquitetura final selecionada para ingestão de eventos é o **Híbrido Causal** (Via 4), implementado ao nível da rede através de um proxy reverso (Traefik).

*   **A Filosofia:** Em vez de depender do *checkout* manual do agente, ou do *snapshotting* de contentores (pesado e opaco), o sistema interceta passivamente as comunicações HTTP entre a interface e os agentes. 
*   **Implementação:** O proxy (Traefik) encaminha as mensagens para o agente executor em tempo real e emite uma cópia (Mirroring) diretamente para o serviço de Ingestão (ligado ao PostgreSQL).
*   **Benefícios:** Torna o historial 100% infalível à disrupção do agente, preservando de forma contínua as chamadas, ferramentas e estado intermédio. Tudo num formato agnóstico e sem acoplamento à implementação final do modelo ou orquestrador.","path":"/home/hugo/office-personal/workbench/agents-systems-memory/agents-network-memory.md"}
